The shared identity key 'project_better-buses-restart' is a broad program label, not an authoritative identity field. Better Buses Restart is a citywide 2021 NYC DOT initiative (project_type: 'bus improvement initiative', status: 'implemented') that launched multiple bus lanes and busways. Jamaica Busway is a specific busway project on Jamaica Avenue and Archer Avenue in Queens (project_type: 'busway', status: 'monitoring') that is explicitly described as 'part of the Better Buses Restart program' in its payload. The Jamaica Busway has its own distinct project_name ('Jamaica Busway'), distinct corridors, distinct launch_date_text ('October 24, 2021'), and distinct relation_context linking to specific corridor records. The colon-notation evidence 'Better Buses Restart: Jamaica Busway' reinforces a parent-program/child-project relationship, not identity equivalence.

The records are related Fordham Road work but not a same-identity duplicate: the first is the broader Fordham/Inwood study and alternatives record, while the second is a later Bronx-only Sedgwick-to-Bronx-River segment/design phase. This is a parent/segment or phase distinction, so the duplicate key should be suppressed with DNM rather than an alias.

Shared program (MTA 2020-2024 Capital Program) is relation context, not merge authority. Identity fields differ: distinct project names, types, and scopes (fire standpipe replacement vs. viaduct repair). Contradiction test: strong identity field project_name and project_type differ, so they are distinct projects.
Shared program is not merge authority. Identity fields differ: project_name, project_type, scope. Contradiction test: strong identity fields differ, so distinct projects.
Shared program is not merge authority. Identity fields differ: project_name, project_type, scope. Contradiction test: distinct projects.

Component/parent distinction: Hall Interlocking Expansion is a component or subproject within Jamaica Capacity Improvements, not the same project identity as the broader JCI program.
Different project_name (strong identity field) - contradicting per test 1. Program is not merge authority.
Different project_name (strong identity field) - contradicting per test 1. Program is not merge authority.
Parent/phase distinction: Phase 2 property-interest actions are within the Jamaica Capacity Improvement effort but should not be collapsed into the broader parent project record.

Agency/scope distinction: the shared 'Security Initiatives' label is generic. LIRR security-camera/electronic-data work and NYCT security command/deployable-camera work are distinct agency-specific initiatives.
Do not merge: the records share only a program/funding context. Program membership is relation context, not project identity, and the project names/scopes differ.
Do not merge: the records share only a program/funding context. Program membership is relation context, not project identity, and the project names/scopes differ.
Different project_name (strong identity field) - contradicting per test 1. Program is not merge authority.

Phase/umbrella distinction: Phase 1 is a bounded design-build contract within the broader Park Avenue Viaduct repair/replacement effort, not the same durable project identity as the overall multi-phase program.
Shared program is not merge authority. Identity fields differ: project_name, project_type. Contradiction test: distinct projects.
Shared program is not merge authority. Identity fields differ: project_name, project_type, scope. Contradiction test: distinct projects.

Program/funding-action versus design-contract distinction: a 2020-2024 Capital Program funding record for Penn Reconstruction is related to, but not identical with, the architectural/engineering design services contract modification.
Shared program is not merge authority. Identity fields differ: project_name, project_type, scope. Contradiction test: distinct projects.

Distinct projects even though both are at Robert F. Kennedy Bridge: different project names ('RK-21Ph2: Fire Standpipe Acceleration Ph.2' vs 'RK-81: Facility Interoperability Improvements (Demo of Old Structure)'), different project numbers (RK-21Ph2 vs RK-81), and different budgets ($4.9M vs $8.8M). They are separate capital acceleration initiatives. Shared program and bridge location do not make them the same project (Test 1: different project scope/name is a strong identity difference).

Distinct projects: different project names and locations (Robert F. Kennedy Bridge vs Verrazzano-Narrows Bridge), different scopes (fire standpipe vs approach reconstruction), different budgets ($4.9M vs $26.1M). Shared program is not merge authority.

Distinct projects: different project names and locations (Robert F. Kennedy Bridge vs Verrazzano-Narrows Bridge), different scopes (facility interoperability improvements vs approach reconstruction), different budgets ($8.8M vs $26.1M). Shared program is not merge authority.

Different capital-program periods and scopes: the 2020-2024 MTA Capital Program is not the 2025-2029 Capital Program. Shared capital-program wording is program-category context, not same project identity.
Distinct annual NYCT committee work plans. The shared work-plan title is recurring document context, while the year is part of the durable project/plan identity.
Distinct annual NYCT committee work plans. The shared work-plan title is recurring document context, while the year is part of the durable project/plan identity.
Distinct annual NYCT committee work plans. The shared work-plan title is recurring document context, while the year is part of the durable project/plan identity.

Same corridor family but distinct community-board/segment scopes. The CB1 and CB2 Victory Boulevard transit-safety records should remain separate scoped project records unless a later source-backed policy explicitly collapses them.

corridor.reason: Both records describe 125th Street in Manhattan, but their canonical corridor limits differ: corridor_125th-st-manhattan is keyed to Morningside Avenue to Second Avenue, while corridor_125th-street-manhattan is keyed to Amsterdam Avenue to Second Avenue. Under the corridor identity canon, street name and borough alone are insufficient and differing limits force a do-not-merge guardrail.

stationary-manhattan is a list of multiple corridors; it has no single street+limits identity. A compound record aggregating many segments is not the same canonical identity as a specific corridor segment. No strong identity fields match.
Same reasoning as above: stationary-manhattan is a collection, not a specific corridor with defined limits. Distinct identities.
Do not merge: the records share a street/corridor label but have different or only partially overlapping limits. Corridor identity requires matching limits/scope.

The two records are fundamentally different in scope and granularity. corridor_archer-ave-busway represents a single specific busway corridor on Archer Avenue with defined limits (153rd St to 160th St eastbound), operational rules (24/7 bus/emergency only), ridership (189K), and bus routes (19). corridor_stationary-queens is an aggregate record listing 14 different Queens streets that had stationary bus lane cameras, functioning as a borough-level index rather than a single physical corridor. The shared street name 'Archer Avenue' is merely one item in a list of 14 streets, not an identity field indicating the same entity. Missing scope (limits, operations, ridership) in stationary-queens and lack of borough-grouping or list semantics in archer-ave-busway confirm they are distinct records serving different purposes.

Whole-corridor/subsegment mismatch: the B82 Southern Brooklyn corridor spans Kings Highway plus Flatlands Avenue and other limits, while the second record is a bounded Kings Highway section. A shared street segment should not collapse the whole corridor into the subsegment.

Whole-corridor/subsegment mismatch. The B82 Southern Brooklyn corridor spans Kings Highway plus Flatlands Avenue and other streets; the other record is a Kings Highway-specific treatment/location record.
Whole-corridor/subsegment mismatch. The CB 11 Kings Highway section is a bounded segment inside the larger B82 Southern Brooklyn corridor, not a same-identity corridor record.

Shared Cropsey Avenue street identity hides a compound Bay Parkway/Cropsey corridor versus a B82 route segment. The broader Bay Parkway/Cropsey study area should not collapse into the B82 Cropsey segment.

Contradiction test: strong identity fields differ. corridor_bedford-avenue-201011 has street='Bedford Avenue' with specific limits 'Between DeKalb Ave and Flushing Ave (bus lane)'; corridor_stationary-brooklyn has a multi-street array (including Bedford Avenue among 13 streets) and no limits. These are not the same canonical corridor. Per-kind rule: a broad study corridor (collection of camera corridors) and a specific segment are distinct.

Shared broad label 'Broadway' is insufficient because these records describe physically distinct corridors in different boroughs (Manhattan vs Queens) with non-overlapping limits (157th–220th St vs 69th St–Roosevelt Ave). The source evidence explicitly distinguishes them by borough, and the only shared identity key is the generic `corridor_broadway` alias, which is a weak label not an authoritative identity field. Borough and limits are strong identity fields that decisively separate these records.
Shared street name Broadway is a weak key. The records are in different boroughs with different physical limits, so this should be a durable do-not-merge suppression.
Different street name (Broadway vs Woodhaven/Cross Bay Boulevards) and different limits/borough scope; strong identity fields 'street' and 'borough' contradict, per Decision Test 1 and corridor rule.
Different boroughs (Queens vs Bronx) per contradictory strong identity field 'borough', per Decision Test 1 and corridor rule.
Different street name (Broadway vs Woodhaven/Cross Bay Boulevards) and different limits; strong identity fields 'street' and 'limits' contradict, per Decision Test 1 and corridor rule.

Decision test 1 (contradiction): strong identity field 'limits' differs. Record 1 has limits 'Bay Pkwy to Ocean Ave and E 23 St to Ave K'; record 2 has limits 'Bay Pkwy to Ocean Ave'. Different limits on same street and borough never merge per corridor rule.
Decision test 1 (contradiction): limits differ. Record 1 has composite limits; record 3 has limits 'at E 34th Street, E 35th Street, and Avenue M' (a point, not a segment).
Decision test 1 (contradiction): limits differ. Record 1 has composite limits including both Bay Pkwy-Ocean Ave and E 23 St-Ave K; record 4 is only the latter segment.
Decision test 1 (contradiction): limits differ. Record 1 has composite limits; record 5 has limits 'Bay Pkwy to McDonald Ave in CB 11'.
Different boroughs (Bronx vs Queens) and different street name; per Decision Test 1 and corridor rule: same street name with differing borough never merges.

Shared Church Avenue street identity hides adjacent but distinct corridor limits: Coney Island Avenue to Flatbush Avenue versus Flatbush Avenue to Ocean Parkway. The pair should be guarded as DNM rather than collapsed.

Sparse Bus Forward 2 Church Avenue SBS-candidate shell lacks limits, while corridor_church-av is the later Flatbush Ave to Ocean Pkwy segment; same street/borough identity key is not enough to merge without explicit source linkage, especially because Bus Forward 2 separately lists an adjacent Church Av local corridor from Coney Island Av to Flatbush Av.

Contradiction test: strong identity fields differ. corridor_buslane-brochure-161st-street has corridor_name '161st Street' with no limits, just a single street example, while corridor_bx6-south-bronx has corridor_name 'South Bronx Crosstown', a defined limits (Riverside Drive West to Hunts Point), and a street list including several streets (161st Street as one component). A specific street segment example is distinct from a multi-street corridor; per corridor identity rule, scope and limits differ, so they are not the same canonical identity.

Physical-extent mismatch: 'north of 165 Av' and discrete draft-plan points at Liberty Avenue and 109 Av are not the same corridor extent. Shared Cross Bay Boulevard street name is too broad for identity.

The records share Cross Bay Boulevard but carry different physical extents: a north-of-165-Av design segment versus the later 107 Av to N Conduit Av bus-lane segment. Shared street name is not enough for corridor identity.
Adjacent/different Cross Bay Boulevard extents should stay distinct: one is north of 165 Av, the other is Rockaway Boulevard to 165 Avenue.
Whole-corridor/subsegment mismatch. A Cross Bay Boulevard segment north of 165 Av should not merge with the full Woodhaven/Cross Bay corridor from Woodside to the Rockaways.

corridor_cross-bay-blvd-liberty-109av-2015 has specific limits 'at Liberty Avenue and 109 Avenue'; corridor_cross-bay-blvd-sbs-spring2015 is a broad study corridor without limits. Per per-kind rule: broad and specific are distinct.
Different limits: 'at Liberty Avenue and 109 Avenue' vs 'between Rockaway Boulevard and 165 Avenue'. Limits contradict (Decision Test 1).
corridor_cross-bay-boulevard-rockaway-to-165 has specific limits; corridor_cross-bay-blvd-sbs-spring2015 is a broad study corridor. Distinct per per-kind rule.
Both are Flatlands Avenue/B82 corridor records, but the limits are different named segments. A durable DNM keeps the street-name key from merging E 103 St to Pennsylvania Ave with Ave I to Ralph Ave.

Shared street name 'Fordham Road' is insufficient because corridor_fordham-road is a single-street segment (Fordham Road, Major Deegan to Boston Road, Bronx) while corridor_pelham-fordham-207 is a multi-street corridor spanning Pelham Parkway, Fordham Road, and West 207 Street from Eastchester Avenue to Broadway — different geographic scope, different limits, and different identity.

Contradiction test: limits differ. `corridor_fulton-st` is a broad study corridor without specified limits, while `corridor_fulton-st-busway` is a specific busway segment with limits 'Adams Street to Flatbush Avenue'. Per corridor decision rules: 'A broad study corridor and a specific segment of it are distinct; relate with subsegment_of, never alias.' Street name and borough alone are insufficient for merge.

Strong identity fields differ: corridor_hylan-boulevard-brt has specific limits (Lincoln Avenue to Steuben Street) and is a single BRT corridor; corridor_stationary-staten-island has no limits and represents a set of stationary camera corridors in Staten Island. Per per-kind rules for corridors, identity = street(s) + borough + limits. The second record lacks limits and its name and description indicate a grouping, not a single scoped corridor, so test 1 (contradiction) applies.
Decision test 1 (contradiction): limits differ. Record 2 is segment 'Bay Pkwy to Ocean Ave'; record 3 is a point location at specific streets.
Decision test 1 (contradiction): limits differ. Record 2 covers west segment (Bay Pkwy to Ocean Ave); record 4 covers east segment (E 23 St to Ave K). They are adjacent but distinct segments.

Decision test 1 (contradiction): limits differ. Record 2 is 'Bay Pkwy to Ocean Ave'; record 5 is 'Bay Pkwy to McDonald Ave' – overlapping but not identical endpoint (Ocean Ave vs McDonald Ave). Different limits.
Decision test 1 (contradiction): limits differ. Record 3 is a point; record 4 is a segment.
Decision test 1 (contradiction): limits differ. Record 3 is a point; record 5 is a segment.
Decision test 1 (contradiction): limits differ. Record 4 is 'E 23 St to Ave K'; record 5 is 'Bay Pkwy to McDonald Ave' – different geographic coverage.
Same Manhattan avenue but non-overlapping corridor limits. A 125th Street/Lexington intersection treatment should not merge with the 96th-to-60th Street bus-forward corridor.
Same Manhattan avenue but clearly distinct limits: 125th Street intersection context versus the 60th-to-52nd Street offset bus lane segment.

Different limits: '96th St to 60th St' vs '60th Street to 52nd Street'. Even though they share the 60th St boundary, they are distinct segments covering different ranges. Per canon, differing borough or limits never merge. Here both Manhattan but limits differ.
Do not merge: the records share a street/corridor label but have different or only partially overlapping limits. Corridor identity requires matching limits/scope.
Both records are on the Montauk Branch, but they are separate bounded branch segments with different endpoints and work scopes.
The records are adjacent but distinct Third Avenue sections. Shared street name should not collapse section-specific corridor records with different limits and section labels.

Do not merge a whole B46/Utica corridor with a bounded St. Johns Place-to-Carroll Street segment. The latter is a segment/variant inside the Utica planning geography, not the same durable corridor identity.

Do not merge the broad B46/Utica corridor with the St. Johns/Carroll-to-Church design segment. They share borough and street and are related in project lifecycle, but the canonical limits and source evidence separate whole-corridor identity from a bounded segment/variant.

The records have different corridor limits: Washington Bridge alone runs from Amsterdam Avenue to University Avenue, while Washington Bridge and West 181 Street runs University Avenue to Broadway. These are partially overlapping but distinct segments, and the corridor_name differs (Washington Bridge vs Washington Bridge and West 181 Street). The shared street 'Washington Bridge' is a weak overlap; the different or partially different limits negative signal is decisive.
Do not merge: the records share a street/corridor label but have different or only partially overlapping limits. Corridor identity requires matching limits/scope.
Same boulevard, different bounded project segments. The shared Woodhaven Boulevard key should not merge Dry Harbor-to-Metropolitan with Union Turnpike-to-81st Road.

The records describe different Woodhaven Boulevard extents. The Dry Harbor-to-Metropolitan bus-lane segment should remain separate from the broader Queens Boulevard-to-107th Avenue corridor/capital-project record.

Different limits: Union Turnpike to 81st Road vs Queens Boulevard to Union Turnpike. The first begins at Union Turnpike and goes south; the second ends at Union Turnpike (or extends to 107th Avenue per display). Distinct corridors.

route.reason: The records preserve different M23 service variants: local crosstown route versus Select Bus Service. Upgrade/replacement context should remain relational, not a route identity merge.

Distinct routes on different streets: route_34th-st-sbs is the 34th Street SBS (Manhattan crosstown on 34th St) while route_m14-ad-sbs is the M14 A/D SBS (14th Street M14A/M14D). The identity key collision (route_34th-st-sbs assigned to both) is an ingestion artifact from the brt_route_index source page listing many SBS routes in one block; both routes happen to appear in the same source block. Payload identity fields confirm different names ('34th Street Select Bus Service' vs 'M14 A Select Bus Service'), different streets ('34th Street' vs '14 St'), and different route numbers (none vs 'M14'). Relation contexts also differ: 34th St SBS has capital improvements in 2014 and off-board fare in 2011, while M14 A/D SBS started in July 2019 on the 14th Street TTP corridor.

Distinct service variants on the M15 corridor. route_able-m15-sbs is the SBS/plus-coded M15+ record, while route_m15-reference is the local/limited M15 record. The source distinguishes the pair as M15/M15 SBS; the shared base route label is corridor context, not same-route identity.
S79 SBS/plus service and historical S79 local service have different service_variant values. Lifecycle or replacement context is not same-route identity.
Shared base label B103 is not enough to merge a proposed limited-stop B103 LTD route with a distinct B103E express route label and express service variant.

Route service variant mismatch: route_b3-2015-sbk-corridor has no determinable variant (unknown), while route_b3-draft-plan is explicitly a Local variant. Per merge canon for routes, different variants never merge with each other or with a variant-unspecified base record by key alone (contradiction test: route service variant differs).

The Phase II B44 record is a generic existing-route/source-context mention for the Nostrand SBS planning corridor, while route_b44-limited is the specific limited-stop service variant. Shared base label B44 is not enough to collapse the generic planning mention into the limited-stop route identity.
Contradiction test: Different service_variant. route_b44-brt-phase2 has no service_variant (unknown) while route_b44-local has local. Variant mismatch forces do_not_merge.

This is a predecessor/planning-context route mention versus the later SBS service variant. The source itself distinguishes existing B44 bus service from planned Nostrand Avenue SBS, so it should not be treated as the same route identity.

Distinct B44 variants: route_b44-limited is the historical limited-stop/pre-SBS service on Nostrand Avenue, while route_b44-local is the local B44 service. Shared base route B44 and stale aliases are not merge authority.

Distinct B44 variants: route_b44-limited is a predecessor limited-stop service, while route_b44-sbs is the Select Bus Service variant. The relationship is predecessor/replacement, not canonical identity.
Distinct B44 variants: route_b44-local is the local bus service, while route_b44-sbs is the Select Bus Service variant. Official plus-id policy maps B44+ to B44-SBS only, not to B44 Local.
B46 local is a single local service variant, while the paired B46 record is a local-and-limited bundled route surface. Bundle-versus-variant records should remain distinct.
Strong identity field service_variant differs: local vs sbs. Test 1 (contradiction test) forces do_not_merge.
The pair is a local/limited predecessor or bundle record versus the B46 SBS route. The shared B46 base label is lifecycle/corridor context, not durable same-route identity.

The pair represents different B82 service variants: existing limited-stop service versus local service. The source context explicitly preserves local stop coverage separately from limited/SBS conversion.
Different service variants (limited_stop vs sbs) on the same base route B82. Per per-kind route rule: different variants never merge.
Shared base label B82 is not enough to merge a limited-stop route with the distinct B82E express service label and express service variant.

Distinct service variants on the same B82 corridor. route_b82-local is the local/TSP-development B82 record, while route_b82-sbs is the B82 SBS record. The shared base route label identifies related corridor context, not same-route identity.

Distinct service variants on the same B82 corridor. route_b82-tsp-2017 is the local/TSP-development B82 record, while route_southern-brooklyn-sbs is the B82 SBS record. The shared base route label identifies related corridor context, not same-route identity.
The records distinguish Q44 SBS from the pre/SBS local-limited Q44 record. Replacement or upgrade context should remain a relation, not a route identity merge.

Different service variants (local vs sbs). Under the Route per-kind rule of the Identity Merge Canon, different variants never merge. The payload identity fields confirm service_variant 'local' for the first record and 'sbs' for the second, which are strong identity fields that differ.
A bundled Bx40/Bx42 corridor-service record should not be collapsed into the single-route Bx42 record just because the bundle contains Bx42.
The pair is a direct local-versus-limited service-variant mismatch on the same base route. The source explicitly treats Bx41 Local and Bx41 Limited as separate services.
The records are predecessor limited-stop service versus the SBS replacement. Replacement evidence is a relation, not proof of same canonical route identity.
Different service variants (local vs sbs). Test 1: strong identity field (service_variant) contradicts, so do_not_merge.

The LGA record is a workshop access-alternatives route idea using the Bx55 label, while the Webster record is the actual/historical Bx55 Limited/Webster service context. They are distinct route/proposal contexts despite sharing the base Bx55 key.

Distinct route variants: Bx6 Local vs Bx6 SBS. The source text explicitly distinguishes them, including Bx6 Local schedule changes being postponed until Bx6 SBS realignment is implemented. Shared base route Bx6 is insufficient to merge different service variants.

Distinct route variants: Bx6 Local vs Bx6 SBS. The source text explicitly distinguishes them ('Bx6 Local schedules...postponed until 2023 when the Bx6 SBS realignment is implemented'). Per instructions, preserve variant distinctions for local vs SBS.
Distinct route variants: Bx6 Local vs Bx6 SBS. Both from the same source document which treats them as separate services. Shared base route BX6 is insufficient to merge different service variants.

The first record is a multi-route corridor (Lexington Ave corridor serving M98, M101, M102, M103 over a 1.8-mile segment from 96th St to 60th St), while the second record is the individual M101 bus route (as used in the ACE program). A corridor spanning multiple routes and a specific route are fundamentally different scopes — corridor is a street-segment planning concept, route is a discrete service line. Shared base route label 'M101' is merely one of several routes served by the corridor and is not a strong identity field indicating the same canonical entity. No evidence shows the corridor record was ever intended to stand for the M101 route alone.
Contradiction test: route_id differs (M101 vs SIM11) and service_variant differs (local vs express). These are distinct routes per identity merge canon.
Contradiction test: route_id differs (M101 vs SIM22) and service_variant differs (local vs express). Distinct routes.
Contradiction test: route_id differs (M101 vs SIM26) and service_variant differs (local vs express). Distinct routes.
Contradiction test: route_id differs (M101 vs SIM6) and service_variant differs (local vs express). Distinct routes.

The Phase II record is a pre-SBS/generic M14 route mention for a potential corridor, while route_m14-ad-sbs is the later Select Bus Service variant. This is predecessor-versus-SBS context, not same route identity.
Test 1 (contradiction): strong identity field service_variant differs — 'local' vs 'limited_stop'. Different variants never merge.
Test 1 (contradiction): strong identity field service_variant differs — 'local' vs 'limited_stop'. Different variants never merge.
Test 1 (contradiction): strong identity field service_variant differs — 'limited_stop' (payload) vs 'local'. Different variants never merge.
Test 1 (contradiction): strong identity field service_variant differs — 'local' vs 'sbs'. Different variants never merge.
Test 1 (contradiction): strong identity field service_variant differs — 'limited_stop' vs 'local'. Different variants never merge.
Test 1 (contradiction): strong identity field service_variant differs — 'limited_stop' vs 'local'. Different variants never merge.
Test 1 (contradiction): strong identity field service_variant differs — 'limited_stop' vs 'local'. Different variants never merge.

Bundled/compound record: route_m15-local-limited agglomerates generic M15 mentions from 6 sources (2009-2010 existing-service references, 2024 segment-speed methodology, 2025 Local/Limited ridership discussion); its limited_stop variant comes from a single merged observation. Per canon bundled-variant rule it must not fold into any single variant. Flagged as a future split candidate, not the same object as the 2010 M15 Limited service.
Test 1 (contradiction): strong identity field service_variant differs — 'limited_stop' vs 'sbs'. Different variants never merge.
Test 1 (contradiction): strong identity field service_variant differs — 'limited_stop' vs 'local'. Different variants never merge.
Test 1 (contradiction): strong identity field service_variant differs — 'limited_stop' vs 'local'. Different variants never merge.
Test 1 (contradiction): strong identity field service_variant differs — 'limited_stop' vs 'local'. Different variants never merge.

Bundled/compound record: route_m15-local-limited agglomerates generic M15 mentions from 6 sources (2009-2010 existing-service references, 2024 segment-speed methodology, 2025 Local/Limited ridership discussion); its limited_stop variant comes from a single merged observation. Per canon bundled-variant rule it must not fold into any single variant. Flagged as a future split candidate, not the same object as the 2010 M15 Limited service.

Test 1 (contradiction): strong identity field service_variant differs — 'limited_stop' vs 'sbs'. Different variants never merge. Despite shared_base_route:M15, the source evidence ('M15 SBS replaces M15 Limited') confirms they are distinct service variants.
Test 1 (contradiction): strong identity field service_variant differs — 'limited_stop' (payload) vs 'local'. Different variants never merge.
Test 1 (contradiction): strong identity field service_variant differs — 'local' vs 'sbs'. Different variants never merge.

Test 1 (contradiction): strong identity field service_variant differs — 'limited_stop' (payload) vs 'local'. Different variants never merge.
Test 1 (contradiction): strong identity field service_variant differs — 'local' vs 'sbs'. Different variants never merge.

Distinct service variants on the M15 corridor. route_m15-sbs is the Select Bus Service record, while route_m15-local-limited is the local/limited M15 record/reference. The source distinguishes the pair as M15/M15 SBS; the shared base route label is corridor context, not same-route identity.
The records are different M34 service variants: local bus versus Select Bus Service. The shared base route key is lifecycle/corridor context, not durable same-route identity.
This is both a branch mismatch and a service-variant mismatch: M34 local is not the M34A SBS branch created from the M16 rename.
M34 SBS and M34A SBS are distinct 34th Street branches. Shared M34/M34A bundle wording is route-list context, not same-route identity.
Contradiction test: strong identity field 'service_variant' differs (local vs sbs). Per route merge canon, different service variants never merge.

Distinct service variants on the M86 corridor. route_m86-local preserves the historical/pre-SBS local route record, while route_m86-sbs is the Select Bus Service route record. The replacement relationship is lifecycle context, not same-route identity.

route_q10-ace is an ACE enforcement grouping of both Q10 and Q80 routes under the combined label 'Q10/Q80' on Lefferts Blvd, as evidenced by the ACE source page listing 'Q10/Q80, Lefferts Blvd (in 60-day warning period)'. route_q10-queens is specifically the Q10 bus route in Queens with multiple service patterns (Kew Gardens-JFK Airport) as described in the Queens bus network redesign. The queens addendum explicitly distinguishes Q10 from Q80 as separate routes. They share the identity key 'route_q10' only because the ACE label contains 'Q10' as part of the composite 'Q10/Q80' designation, which is insufficient evidence for merging distinct canonical routes.

Official 2025 Queens Bus Network Redesign evidence ends Q15A while continuing Q15 over part of the former Q15A routing. Shared routing is lifecycle continuity, not authority to merge the discontinued Q15A route identity with the current Q15 route identity.

Official 2025 Queens Bus Network Redesign evidence says Q20A ended and was replaced by a newly started Q20 that mostly follows the same routing. Replacement and routing inheritance are lifecycle relations, not same-route identity.

Q20A and Q20B are separately named historical route branches with separate canonical route identities and distinct service-end rows in the official 2025 redesign source. Older Q20A/B bundle literals are context, not merge authority.
Do not merge: the packet carries a route contradiction (different route_id, service variant, or source route context). A shared base-route string is not merge authority for distinct route records.

The official 2025 Queens Bus Network Redesign row explicitly distinguishes the existing Q48, which was discontinued and replaced by Q90, from the new Q48, which began serving the former Q46 Glen Oaks branch. Reuse of the Q48 designation across that discontinuation and replacement is a route-lifecycle discontinuity, not evidence that the historical LaGuardia route and new Glen Oaks route are one canonical route.

The proposed Q52/53 SBS record is a combined corridor/route-bundle surface, while route_q52-sbs-queens is the individual Q52 SBS route branch. Bundle-versus-branch should remain distinct rather than aliasing the combined Q52/53 proposal to Q52 alone.

The LGA workshop Q53 record is an airport-access route idea, while route_q53-sbs-ace is the actual Q53 SBS service on the Woodhaven/Cross Bay corridor. Shared Q53 numbering is not sufficient for route identity.
Do not merge: the packet carries a route contradiction (different route_id, service variant, or source route context). A shared base-route string is not merge authority for distinct route records.
SIM23 is an individual express route, while SIM23/SIM24 is a bundled express-route scope record. Shared SIM23 route number is insufficient for merging a branch with an aggregate bundle.

Bx18A is a GTFS-addressable branch, while Bx18A/B is the official evaluation's aggregate reporting surface for both Bronx branches. Preserve the aggregate identity for aggregate pilot metrics and do not alias it to one branch.

Bx18B is a GTFS-addressable branch, while Bx18A/B is the official evaluation's aggregate reporting surface for both Bronx branches. Preserve the aggregate identity for aggregate pilot metrics and do not alias it to one branch.

S46 is the GTFS-addressable local route, while S46/S96 is the official evaluation's aggregate reporting surface for the Staten Island local and limited services. Preserve the aggregate identity for aggregate pilot metrics and do not alias it to S46.

S96 is the GTFS-addressable limited route, while S46/S96 is the official evaluation's aggregate reporting surface for the Staten Island local and limited services. Preserve the aggregate identity for aggregate pilot metrics and do not alias it to S96.
